title: Chapter 7: Greg's Suspicions
content: Greg wouldn't let it go.

This was, in retrospect, entirely predictable. Greg was the kind of person who treated "letting things go" as a character flaw — something weak people did to avoid accountability. Greg didn't avoid. Greg *pursued.* Greg chased things down long after any reasonable person would have stopped, shrugged, and gotten lunch. Greg was a dog with a bone, except the bone was your personal business and the dog had an MBA and a LinkedIn Premium account.

It had started three days after Chloe's medical leave email. The email had been Dave's idea — careful, vague, just professional enough. "Severe fur condition. Extended medical leave. Will update." It was the kind of email that raised questions but discouraged follow-up, the corporate equivalent of closing your eyes and hoping the monster goes away.
Greg had called six times.

He'd emailed four times, each message more pointedly concerned than the last, like concern was a blade and he was testing the edge. He'd DM'd on LinkedIn — *LinkedIn,* the most unhinged place on the internet, where professionals went to perform interest in each other's thought leadership like actors in a community theater production of *Glengarry Glen Ross.* His message was three words: "Checking in. 💼"

The briefcase emoji. The *briefcase emoji.* As if the performative professionalism of a tiny yellow briefcase could disguise the fact that Greg was sniffing around like a man who suspected something and wanted to be the first to know what it was.
"He's not checking in," Maya said, hanging up after the sixth call. "He's circling."
"Circling?" Elif looked up from her phone, where she was designing a custom birth chart for someone named Skyler.
"Like a shark. Like a lawyer. Like a guy who knows you left something off your taxes and he's deciding whether it's worth the audit."

Chloe, from the couch, felt a ripple run through her fur. It was an unfamiliar sensation — like goosebumps, but slower, deeper, traveling in a wave from the crown of her head down her spine to the tip of her tail. Her hum, which was usually a warm and tuneless baritone, went flat. Not silent. Just... wrong. A record playing at the wrong speed.
She didn't like this.

She didn't like Greg. She hadn't liked Greg when she was a human and he was her boss, and she didn't like him now, from the other side of a species barrier, with the added clarity of an animal that could sense threat the way a barometer senses weather. Greg was wrong. He wasn't evil — evil was dramatic, evil had style — he was just *wrong,* the way a spreadsheet with a single misplaced decimal was wrong. Quietly, boringly, catastrophically wrong.
The apartment landline rang again.
Everyone stared at it.
"His number?" Elif asked.
Dave checked the caller ID. Nodded.
Maya picked up. "Hello?"
"Maya! It's Greg. From Whitfield & Associates. Chloe's work."

His voice was warm. Greg's voice was always warm. Greg's voice had been calibrated in some hellish laboratory to sound like a guy you wanted to trust — confident, friendly, just concerned enough to seem human. It was a voice that said *I'm on your side* while its hands were going through your pockets.
"She's recovering," Maya said.
"From *what?*"
She could hear him smiling. Not a nice smile. A *gotcha* smile. The smile of a man who knew that "severe fur condition" was weird and was enjoying being the one who got to be weird about it.
"Fur," Maya said, and hung up.
She put the phone down. Looked at Chloe.
"He's going to show up here," she said.
It wasn't a question.
"All due respect," Dave said, from his corner of the couch, where he was wrapped in a blanket like a sleep-deprived burrito, "how? We have a buzzer."

"He'll follow someone in. He'll time it. He'll stand by the door and catch it when someone leaves. Greg is — and I say this with full knowledge of how insane it sounds — *strategically shameless.* He will do things that a normal person would find socially impossible, and he will do them without flinching, because he has never once in his life experienced social consequences."
Elif nodded slowly. "I've met guys like that. They're all named Greg or something adjacent. Brent. Chad. Trevor."
"Greg," Dave confirmed.
Chloe stood up from the couch. This was notable because Chloe almost never stood up from the couch without a very good reason, and the reason was usually food. But now she was up, and she was pacing.
Pacing.

Back and forth across the living room rug, her paws making soft thuds on the hardwood, her body a hundred and fifty pounds of barely contained anxiety moving in a tight, compulsive loop. Past the coffee table. Past Elif's crystals. Past the spot where the sunbeam would be in two hours. Back again. Again.
This was new.

Capybaras did not pace. Capybaras were the most relaxed animals on the planet — the kind of creatures that let birds sit on their heads and turtles nap on their backs, the kind of animals that looked at the entire concept of stress and said "no thank you." Chloe's body was literally, physiologically, evolutionarily designed to not feel stress. Her nervous system was a warm bath. Her hormones were a lullaby. She was a biological monument to chilling out.

And Greg — just the idea of Greg, just the name, just the memory of fluorescent lights and performance reviews and the way Greg said "team player" like it meant "person who doesn't complain when I make them work weekends" — was making her pace.

Her hum was flat, off-key, a record skipping. Her fur was prickled along her spine. Her eyes had that wide, skittish look that capybaras got when they sensed a predator, except the predator was a forty-five-year-old man in business casual who really wanted to know why his employee had said "fur condition."
"Chloe," Elif said softly. "Hey. Come here."

Chloe didn't come. She paced. Back and forth. The old anxiety was there, the one she thought she'd left behind with her human body — the churn, the spiral, the feeling of being trapped in a box that kept getting smaller. It was different now, muted by the capybara biology, but it was there. A tug-of-war inside her chest between what her body wanted (calm, stillness, grass, water) and what her mind remembered (deadlines, expectations, the way Greg's voice dropped half an octave when he was about to make you feel small).

"Your body was literally designed to not feel stress," Elif said, sitting on the floor cross-legged, watching Chloe pace. "And this guy is making you stressed anyway. That's medical-level toxic. Like, if stress was a disease and your immune system was a capybara — which it literally is — Greg would be the one pathogen getting through. He's autoimmune stress."
"Elif," Maya said, "that's the most convoluted metaphor you've ever produced."
"It's also the most accurate. Don't @ me."

Chloe stopped pacing. She stood in the middle of the room, sides heaving just slightly — a big, barrel-shaped animal with her heart doing something it shouldn't be able to do, beating too fast in a body that was built to never let that happen.
Maya sat down on the floor next to Elif. They both looked at Chloe.

"Hey," Maya said. "He's not getting in here. Okay? He's not. I don't care how strategically shameless he is. This apartment has a buzzer, a lock, and three people who would rather die than let some corporate ghoul stress out our capybara."
Chloe made a sound. It was small and warbly and not at all the deep, contented hum she usually produced. It was the capybara equivalent of a shaky breath.
"We're going to figure this out," Maya said. "But right now, you need to not pace a trench into the floor."
Chloe looked at her. Then she sat down. Heavily. All at once, like a beanbag chair being dropped. The impact rattled Elif's crystals.
"Good," Maya said. "That's good."
That night, Chloe couldn't sleep.

This was extraordinary. Before the transformation, insomnia had been her constant companion — she'd spent three years staring at the ceiling of her studio apartment at 3 AM, running through every emails she'd ever sent, looking for the one that would get her fired. But as a capybara, she'd slept like a champion. Like an Olympian of rest. Like sleep was her sport and she was going for gold.
Not tonight.

She lay on the living room rug, eyes open, staring at the ceiling. It was a boring ceiling — white, cracked in places, with a water stain that looked like a duck if you squinted and a face if you squinted harder. She thought about Greg. She thought about the office. She thought about the open-plan desk where she'd sat for eight hours a day, fielding calls from journalists who hated her clients and emails from Greg that said things like "Just circling back! 😊" and "Per my last email" and "I know it's 11 PM but can you—"
Her hum was silent. Completely silent. The first time since the transformation.
She heard a noise from the hallway. Footsteps. Then the sound of the ladder from the hallway closet being opened, then dragged.
Then paint. The smell of paint.
Chloe lifted her head.
Maya was in the living room, standing on the ladder, a can of paint in one hand and a brush in the other. It was — Chloe looked at the window — it was two in the morning.
"What are you doing?" Elif asked, stumbling out of her room, one eye closed, hair a chaotic halo.
"Painting," Maya said.
"I can see that. Why?"
Maya didn't answer. She dipped her brush and reached up to the ceiling, making a long, sweeping stroke — blue, a soft warm blue, the blue of sky that existed only in memory and very good weather.
Chloe watched, mesmerized.

Maya painted in silence. She didn't turn on music. She didn't put on a podcast. She just painted — broad strokes for sky, careful dabs for clouds, the kind of clouds that looked like they were moving, like they were on their way somewhere and didn't mind if you watched.

She was good. Chloe had always known Maya was good — the illustrations on the walls, the occasional freelance job that made Maya curse about deadlines but produce work that made people say *oh, wow* — but she'd never watched her work. Not like this. Not in the middle of the night with paint fumes and silence and the kind of focus that looked like meditation.
"Why the ceiling?" Elif asked, sitting on the couch, pulling a blanket over her legs, apparently deciding that if this was happening, she was at least going to be comfortable.
Maya paused. Looked down at Chloe. Back up at the ceiling.
"Because if she's going to stare at the ceiling," Maya said, "it should be interesting."
Chloe's hum started again.

Soft at first. Uncertain. A note that wasn't quite in tune, searching for its pitch. Then it found it — a low, warm tone that started in her chest and filled the room, the way the morning filled the sky on the ceiling above her.

Maya painted until the sky stretched from one end of the living room to the other. Clouds that drifted. A suggestion of sun at the edge, golden, just rising. And in one corner, barely visible, easy to miss if you weren't looking — a tiny bird. Sitting on a cloud. Looking down.
"There," Maya said, stepping off the ladder at 4:17 AM.
She had paint on her arms, her knees, a streak across her forehead. She looked exhausted and satisfied in equal measure.
Chloe looked up at the ceiling. The sky looked back.

It was beautiful. Not museum beautiful, not Instagram beautiful — *real* beautiful. The kind of beautiful that only existed when someone made something for one specific person, in one specific room, at four in the morning, because they didn't know what else to do but make the ceiling less empty.

Chloe lay back down. She stared at the clouds. Her hum steadied, deepened, found its rhythm again — the warm, round tone that meant she was okay, or at least that her body had decided to be okay on her behalf, which was close enough.

The tug-of-war inside her was still there. The old anxiety was pulling. Greg was pulling. The fluorescent-lit memory of performance reviews and passive-aggressive check-ins and the phrase "circle back" was pulling.
But capybara calm was pulling too. Heavy, slow, relentless. The gravitational calm of an animal that had evolved over millions of years to lie in water and not think about emails.
And now there was a painted sky on the ceiling, which was also pulling.
Three against one. The math was starting to work out.
The next morning, Dave emerged from his room, took one look at the ceiling, and said, "Cool."
"That's it?" Elif said. "Cool?"
"What do you want me to say? It's a sky. On the ceiling. It's cool."
"You're impossible to impress."
"You try impressing someone who once watched a nuclear reactor shutdown simulation for fun."
"That's not— when did you— Dave, what?"
But Dave had already gone to make coffee, which was his way of expressing the full range of human emotion.
Maya came out of her room twenty minutes later, paint still in her hair. She looked at the ceiling in the daylight, squinted critically at one cloud, and said, "That one's a little lopsided."
"It's perfect," Elif said.
"It's lopsided."
"Maya. It's perfect. Say it's perfect."
"...It's fine."

Chloe lay on the rug, looking up. The morning light from the window caught the edge of the painted sun and made it glow, just slightly, just enough. The clouds looked like they were breathing. The tiny bird on its cloud looked down at her with the quiet confidence of something that had chosen its perch and was content with it.
Her hum was steady again. Not silent. Not flat. Just — right. The baseline frequency of a capybara who was, despite everything, still here.
Greg was still out there. Six calls, four emails, a LinkedIn DM with a briefcase emoji. Greg who wouldn't let go. Greg who orbited.
But in here, the ceiling was a sky, and her roommates were closing ranks around her like walls, like arms, like the kind of silence that meant *we've got you.*
Chloe closed her eyes.

The hum filled the apartment, warm and even, and if you listened closely — which Mrs. Hendricks might have, if she'd been standing in the hallway with her clipboard — it sounded almost like someone saying, without words, without language, without anything but the vibration of a body that had decided to be okay:
*Thank you.*
